content:
We found out from a customer. I’d rather not do that again.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Jakub Żerdzicki on UnsplashWe hit 92% code coverage last month. Dashboard was green everywhere. I felt good about it — we’d been pushing test discipline hard and the numbers were finally there.
Then a bug got to production that three separate test suites should have caught.
Not some obscure edge case. A data transformation that returned garbage when a field was null. Embarrassingly basic. The kind of thing you catch in your first pass if you’re paying attention.
So I went and read the tests. Not skimmed — actually read them line by line. That’s when things started to unravel.
What the tests were actually doing
Most of the test files had been AI-generated. Engineers were either letting Copilot autocomplete them or pasting functions into ChatGPT and asking for unit tests. And the output was fast — a function that would’ve taken twenty minutes to test properly had a full test file in under two minutes.
But the tests just confirmed that the code does what the code does.
The AI looked at the function, figured out its behavior, and wrote assertions that matched. Which sounds right until you think about it for…
